FT_IC1.title: Screen Time Use Among US Adolescents During the COVID-19 Pandemic: Findings From
FT_IC1.c1_evidence: Paper does not explicitly state ABCD study membership in title or abstract. Text indicates data are from 'May 2020 COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release' and references the ABCD Study, but the letter format and phrasing ('This cross-sectional study reviews findings from...') suggests secondary analysis of ABCD data, not primary ABCD participation by these authors. The study is published as a Letter in JAMA Pediatrics, not a full ABCD study report. Per FT-IC1, must be original ABCD analysis or clear US ABCD sub-sample work; this reads as a selective reporting letter rather than an ABCD consortium or ABCD-participating team analysis.
FT_IC1.c2_evidence: ABCD Study, n=5412 US adolescents aged 10-14, May 2020 COVID-19 survey

FT_IC1.title: Evaluating hypothetical prevention strategies for internalizing symptoms in the
FT_IC1.c1_evidence: Pooled analysis combining ABCD (N=8,699) with Generation R (Dutch cohort, N=3,739). No US-ABCD-only estimates reported. Per binding rule: pooled analysis with no US-ABCD-only subset fails FT-IC1.
FT_IC1.c2_evidence: ABCD Study data explicitly mentioned; pooled analysis with ABCD and Generation R, but paper reports ABCD-specific estimates separately per target trial emulation framework

FT_IC1.title: Leveraging the adolescent brain cognitive development study to advance and promo
FT_IC1.c1_evidence: ABCD Study special issue introduction; mentions US ABCD Study and 21 recruitment sites across United States
FT_IC1.c2_evidence: This is an introduction/editorial to a special issue, not a research article analyzing ABCD data. Discusses multiple papers in the special issue but does not itself report original ABCD analyses.

FT_IC1.title: Multiâlevel predictors of depression symptoms in the Adolescent Brain Cognitiv
FT_IC1.c1_evidence: Title mentions ABCD study but no PDF available to confirm full dataset details, sample size, or release version.
FT_IC1.c2_evidence: ABCD study (referenced in abstract and methods)

FT_IC1.title: Bidirectional associations of problematic social media use and problematic gamin
FT_IC1.c1_evidence: Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort study from the Netherlands, not the US ABCD Study. Explicitly stated: 'This longitudinal study started in 2003-2004 when pregnant women living in Amsterdam were invited to participate in the cohort study'
FT_IC1.c2_evidence: Abstract mentions 'ABCD study' and 'ABCD-youth' but does not explicitly confirm US ABCD Study or clarify if this is using ABCD data vs. citation only. Requires full text confirmation.

FT_IC5.title: Screen Time Use Among US Adolescents During the COVID-19 Pandemic: Findings From
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Paper is a Letter (not full research article) and explicitly states 'This cross-sectional study reviews findings from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.' This is secondary analysis/reporting of ABCD data, not original ABCD empirical analysis by ABCD team. Per B14, letter/review format combined with secondary reporting fails FT-IC5.
FT_IC5.c2_evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data with SM→outcome relationships examined

FT_IC5.title: The association between adverse childhood experiences (ACEs), bullying victimiza
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data with regression models predicting mental health outcomes.

FT_IC5.title: The Association between Family Environment and Subsequent Risk of Cyberbullying
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with prospective longitudinal design. Data collected at Year 1 (family environment measures) and Year 2 (cyberbullying victimization outcome), with 10,410 participants included in analyses.

FT_IC5.title: Prevalence and Patterns of Social Media Use in Early Adolescents.
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data using descriptive methods (Chi-square, t-tests) on cross-sectional Year 3 follow-up data.
FT_IC5.c2_evidence: Pure descriptive/prevalence study; no SM↔non-SM-outcome analysis. Applies B14: descriptive data collection on platform use and prevalence patterns only. No inferential test of SM predicting an external outcome.

FT_IC5.title: Youth screen use in the ABCDÂ® study.
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD study. Measurement paper demonstrating screen usage questionnaire development, longitudinal changes baseline–Year 2, and psychometric analyses of SMAQ/VGAQ
FT_IC5.c2_evidence: B13: Pure measurement/concordance paper. Adapted SMAQ and VGAQ scales, demonstrated psychometric properties across populations. No original SM → outcome analysis. Not eligible under B13 (would require at least one SM ↔ non-SM coefficient).

FT_IC5.title: Radiofrequency electromagnetic fields, screen time, and emotional and behavioura
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Population is Amsterdam ABCD (Dutch), not U.S. ABCD Study. Not eligible for inclusion.
FT_IC5.c2_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of 3102 children from the cohort with multiple exposure and outcome measurements.

FT_IC5.title: Cyberbullying, mental health, and substance use experimentation among early adol
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Not assessed due to FT_IC2 NOT_MET status
FT_IC5.c2_evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis on ABCD cohort

FT_IC5.title: Bidirectional associations of problematic social media use and problematic gamin
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis from ABCD (Amsterdam) cohort with two time points (T1: 2019-2020; T2: 2021)
FT_IC5.c2_evidence: Abstract suggests empirical analysis but unclear if original ABCD analysis or secondary analysis of existing data without new coefficients

FT_IC5.title: Traumatic Stress Exposure as a Predictor of Dual Systems Model Development: Exam
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis. Prospective longitudinal SEM examining direct and indirect effects of traumatic stress exposure on dual systems model constructs using data from Waves 1, 3, 4, and 5.

FT_IC5.title: Associations between media parenting practices and early adolescent consumption
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis examining associations between parental media practices and adolescent mature-content consumption

FT_IC5.title: Passive Sensing of Preteens' Smartphone Use: An Adolescent Brain Cognitive Devel
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Applies B12 + B14 carve-out. This is a measurement paper comparing self-reported vs objective smartphone assessment. No original analysis of SM predicting external outcomes. No SM ↔ non-SM outcome coefficient. Feasibility/acceptability focus only.
FT_IC5.c2_evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study data. Pilot substudy with original data collection (passive sensing of smartphone use via EARS app over 4 weeks) and original analysis of app use patterns and measurement validation.

FT_IC5.title: Typologies of Family Functioning and 24-h Movement Behaviors.
FT_IC5.c1_evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis using latent profile analysis on baseline cross-sectional data to examine family typologies and movement behaviors

FT_IC2.title: Associations of Screen Time and Physical Activity With Body Mass Index in Early
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Self-reported recreational screen time (comprehensive measure: TV, videos, games, texting, SM, video chat) measured via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Qualifies as time on screen modality per FT-IC2; covers smartphones/SM per B1.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time only (no per-item breakdown of platform/app/content). Reports total daily screen time (6.1±5.2 h/day) without distinguishing smartphone, tablet, TV, or gaming platform. B2 applies: summed/total screen time with no per-item breakdown.

FT_IC2.title: Prospective associations between media parenting practices and adolescent video
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Video game use (mature-rated, problematic, weekend time); qualifies per B6 (mobile/app gaming). Outcomes measured: frequency of mature-rated games, VGAQ (problematic use), total weekend game time.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Video game use only (gaming without social media or smartphone time). No qualifying SM variable (time on social media, platforms, etc.). No separate SM measure. B6 inapplicable: gaming alone without SM coefficient/separate measurement. Per B5, unspecified gaming (without SM) does not meet criteria.

FT_IC2.title: Associations Between Adverse Childhood Experiences, Adolescent Screen Time and P
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Screen time qualifying variable (self-reported hours across 7 modalities: gaming, texting, social media, video chat, internet browsing, streaming video). Applies B2 (per-item breakdown of screen time reported, not summed-only)
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Paper examines ACEs as IV predicting screen time as outcome. Screen time is measured (self-reported hours across 7 modalities: gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, internet browsing, watching TV/movies) but is the DEPENDENT variable, not an exposure. No qualifying SM/smartphone variable as predictor of a non-SM outcome. Applies B7 (screen time measured but not as exposure per inclusion criteria).

FT_IC2.title: The association between screen time and cognitive function in children: The part
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Screen time (total and by digital device: television, videos, video games, texting, video chat) as independent variable. Meets B1 (total screen time reported; also examined by device). Applies B1 rule.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time measured as total aggregate across unspecified platforms (television, videos, video games, texting, video chat) without separate per-item coefficients in main analyses. Main results report only total screen time β; represents internet use without device/platform specificity per B5. Fails B2 (only summed/total screen time, no per-item breakdown in main results).

FT_IC2.title: Identification and Validation of Distinct Latent Neurodevelopmental Profiles in
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Paper reports screen time as a comparative variable but does NOT use qualifying smartphone/SM exposure as primary variable. Screen time is measured and compared by demographic profiles, but there is no separate measurement of smartphone ownership, app use, texting, SM platforms, or qualifying scales (MPIQ, PSMUS, SMAQ, SNS-A, Bergen). Per B5, general screen time without device/platform specificity does not meet FT-IC2. Measurement is latent profile analysis of fMRI brain activation in reward, inhibition, and emotion regulation domains.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time measured; abstract states 'more screen time' as comparative finding across neurodevelopmental profiles (B5 qualifier: screen time is qualifying variable; applies B1)

FT_IC2.title: Current, future and potential use of mobile and wearable technologies and social
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Resource/technology review paper, not empirical measurement paper. Discusses mobile and wearable technologies and social media data but does NOT report any primary measurements or uses of qualifying SM/smartphone variables in original ABCD data analysis. Paper outlines 'current, future and potential use' of technologies; no extractable SM exposure variables from ABCD data analyzed here. Per FT-IC5 binding (B14), resource/data papers are excluded as NOT empirical analysis.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Mobile phone use, smartphone technologies, wearable devices (accelerometers, Fitbit), social media data, and passive sensing methods discussed as current/future data collection approaches in ABCD.

FT_IC2.title: Youth screen use in the ABCDÂ® study.
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Social media (SMAQ—6-item Bergen Social Media Addiction Scale adapted Year 2); Video gaming (VGAQ—8-item Bergen Video Game Addiction Questionnaire Year 2); time on social media; online social activity; video gaming time. Qualifies under B1 (YSTS with separate SM/gaming coefficients) and B6 (gaming + SM with separate coefficients)
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: B12: Pure measurement paper with psychometric analyses (SMAQ/VGAQ factor structure, invariance testing across demographics). Descriptive statistics only (means, comparisons by sex/race). No SM or smartphone as IV with coefficient predicting external outcome.

FT_IC2.title: NowIKnowMyABCD: A global resource hub for researchers using data from the ABCD S
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Resource/documentation paper does not report data on specific smartphone or SM variables
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Paper is a resource hub describing support infrastructure for ABCD researchers. No smartphone or social media data analysis. Discussion board mentions SM in a live feed from Twitter but does not report SM or smartphone measurements or variables in original analysis. B14 applies.

FT_IC2.title: Outdoor and indoor sources of residential radiofrequency electromagnetic fields,
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Qualifying smartphone variables: personal cell phone use and cordless phone use (both reported as continuous exposure measures)
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Unclear at abstract level. Exposes measured include 'personal cell phone and cordless phone use' and 'residential RF-EMF exposure.' RF-EMF exposure is NOT qualifying (falls under B7: RF-EMF → NOT_MET). Phone use would be qualifying if specified as smartphone/app/texting/video chat, but abstract only says 'cell phone and cordless phone use' without further detail. Requires PDF review for specificity.

FT_IC2.title: Prospective Predictors of Adolescent Screen Time and Problematic Screen Use.
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Qualifying smartphone/app and social media variables. Screen time operationalized as separate measurements: average daily video game hours, average daily social media hours, smartphone/smartwatch ownership. B1 applies: YSTS paper with separate β/OR for video games, social media, and smartphone measured independently with own coefficients reported (Table 3 and Table 4)
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time (ST) and problematic screen use (PSU) are DEPENDENT VARIABLES (outcomes being predicted), not exposures. The paper examines whether baseline impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, and cognitive ability PREDICT screen time/PSU in mid-adolescence, not the reverse.

FT_IC2.title: Developmental Pathways From Childhood Neurodevelopmental Traits to Early Adolesc
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Paper lists 'screen use' as a lifestyle mediator variable, measured as 'duration of screen use (distinguishing between social media, social engagement, passive watching, and active gaming)'. However, screen use is unspecified-platform generic internet time without breakdown to smartphone/app OR social media specifically as IV. Per B5 (unspecified-platform 'internet use' without device/platform → NOT_MET) and B7 (generic screen time without specific SM/smartphone focus → NOT_MET). Neurodevelopmental traits are the IV; psychiatric outcomes are DVs. Screen use is tested as a MEDIATOR (path), not as the primary IV. No standalone SM↔outcome coefficient reported; screen use only appears in mediation analyses. Does not meet exposure criterion for this review.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Social media explicitly included as distinct lifestyle factor in analyses (Figure 2, Figure 3, and text: 'duration of screen use (distinguishing between social media, social engagement, passive watching, and active gaming)'); social media is qualifying per definition of platform-specific/general social media use

FT_IC2.title: 24-Hour Movement Behaviors and Internalizing and Externalizing Behaviors Among Y
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Screen time measured as recreational screen-based activities (TV, video watching, video gaming, texting, social media, video chat) but NOT qualifying as smartphone/social media exposure per binding rules. Paper groups screen time with physical activity and sleep as a composite health guideline metric, not examining SM or smartphone use independently as a qualifying variable. Texting and social media are included in a summed screen-time measure but no separate per-item coefficient reported (B2 rule applies—total/summed screen time only, no per-item breakdown).
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Qualifying smartphone/screen time variable: recreational screen time measured via Youth Screen Time Survey, including TV watching, video watching (YouTube), video game playing, texting on cellphone/tablet/computer, visiting social media sites, and video chat (page 3). Applies B1 (separate coefficient for screen time) and B5 (screen time measured separately from unspecified internet/gaming).

FT_IC2.title: The Pandemic's Toll on Young Adolescents: Prevention and Intervention Targets to
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Mentions 'screen time' as predictor of youth distress (Results, Figure 2), and 'time spent on social media' in Results. However, no qualifying smartphone or platform-specific social media variable with separate coefficient is reported. Screen time without device/platform specification (B5) and generic 'screen time' do not meet criteria. Unclear whether social media use is measured separately or bundled in aggregate screen time.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time (pandemic-era) as predictor. Reported in feature importance: 'screen time variables' (e.g., 'watching news related to coronavirus', 'playing single-player video games', 'browsing the internet'). Qualifies under smartphone/SM use as predictor variable.

FT_IC2.title: Identification and characterization of screen use trajectories from late childho
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: B2 rule: only summed/total screen time reported (TV + videos + games + social media + video chat + texting combined as 'average daily screen use'); no per-item breakdown with separate coefficients anywhere in paper
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Qualifying screen use variable: average daily screen use calculated as sum of self-reported hours of television, videos, video games, social media, video chat, and texting (Bagot et al., 2018). Applies B5 (total screen time summed) but includes separate components. Growth mixture modeling examines trajectories of screen use as primary outcome.

FT_IC2.title: Demographic, psychological, behavioral, and cognitive correlates of BMI in youth
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Screen time defined as 'average minutes per week on computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device' - unspecified platform, generic internet use without device/platform breakdown. Per B5 and B7, unspecified-platform use does NOT meet criteria.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time (average minutes per week on computer, cellphone, tablet, or other electronic device) — qualifying smartphone/screen variable; B8 applies: penalized regression (LASSO/Ridge/Elastic net) with traditional regression coefficients reported in main text and figures

FT_IC2.title: Evaluating hypothetical prevention strategies for internalizing symptoms in the
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Screen time (ST) measured at age 10, primary exposure. Qualifies under B5 exclusion scope: while TV is mentioned, ST is measured as general screen time inclusive of multiple activities including video/media. Paper reports ST as qualifying exposure for internalizing outcomes analysis.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time is summed/total construct only; paper measures ST, PA, sleep, social support but does not break down qualifying smartphone/social media variables with own coefficients. ST is measured as composite with no per-item breakdown. Applies B2.

FT_IC2.title: Longitudinal relationships between lifestyle risk factors and neurodevelopment i
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Screen time included but measured ONLY as part of a summed/latent lifestyle risk factor domain (sleep, physical activity, BMI, screen time). No separate coefficient reported for screen time as an independent variable. Violates B2: 'YSTS papers reporting only summed/total screen time with no per-item breakdown anywhere → NOT_MET'
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time (Youth Screen Time Survey, self-reported recreational screen time on weekdays/weekends) — qualifies as smartphone/screen exposure per B5 (not TV-only). Modeled as component of lifestyle risk factor latent domain. NOT cyberbullying-only per B3.

FT_IC2.title: Leveraging the adolescent brain cognitive development study to advance and promo
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Introduction/editorial paper; does not report primary empirical data on SM/smartphone variables. Mentions 'screen time' in overview but no specific qualifying SM or smartphone measure analyzed in this paper itself
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Not applicable - this is a commentary/introduction paper, not an empirical research study.

FT_IC2.title: One-year predictions of delayed reward discounting in the adolescent brain cogni
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Screen media activity included as predictor variable in machine learning models. Abstract lists 'screen media activity' as key predictive variable. Qualifies as SM/smartphone exposure variable per B1.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Abstract mentions 'screen media activity' as a key predictive variable but does not specify whether this is a qualifying smartphone or SM measure. No named scale or specific platform mentioned. Insufficient detail to confirm FT_IC2 without full paper text; likely refers to screen time aggregation rather than specific SM or smartphone variable per B2 (summed screen time without per-item breakdown)

FT_IC2.title: Generalizable prediction of childhood ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive testing
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: B5 rule: 'screen time' mentioned only as a covariate/feature in machine learning models predicting ADHD symptoms. Screen time is one of many demographic and child characteristic inputs (alongside neurocognition, sex, impulsivity, parental monitoring, etc.) used in predictive models. No separate smartphone or social media exposure analysis; no independent investigation of SM↔non-SM outcomes. Screen time is included as one of multiple features but is not the focus of exposure investigation
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time included as a predictor variable in machine learning models. Methods section states: 'Features from multiple domains contributed meaningfully to prediction, including neurocognition, sex, self-reported impulsivity, parental monitoring, and screen time.' Screen time is a qualifying smartphone/device use variable (self-reported hours of engagement). Applies B8: machine learning with traditional regression baseline validation (PCR and LASSO models both used).

FT_IC2.title: Independent and Joint Prospective Associations of Screen Time and Sleep Disturba
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Screen time (hours/day) reported but without device/platform specificity. Sleep disturbance and sleep duration are not qualifying exposures. Per B5, unspecified screen time without smartphone/app/platform breakdown = NOT_MET
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time is qualifying smartphone exposure measured in hours/day (continuous and categorical: 0-4, >4-8, >8 hours/day). Self-reported average daily time on screens. Separate β coefficients reported for categorical screen time variables (Table 2: B=0.32 continuous, separate models for medium/high categories). Applies B1: ABCD paper reporting separate coefficients for screen time (smartphone-related measure) as exposure.

FT_IC2.title: Prospective associations between sleep, sensation-seeking, and mature screen usa
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Mature screen usage assessed as: mature-rated video game playing (yes/no) and R-rated movie watching frequency (4-point scale: never/once in a while/regularly/all the time). Does not measure qualifying smartphone or social media variables per FT-IC2 binding rules. Mature content consumption is not a qualifying variable (B5 equivalent: reading-on-screens/content type rather than device/platform use). Sleep and sensation-seeking are the predictors, not screen devices/platforms.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Mature screen usage measured via self-report: mature-rated video games and R-rated movies assessed at year 3. Applies B1: separate coefficients for mature video gaming and R-rated movie watching reported in same models (e.g., 'Shorter sleep duration...were associated with mature-rated video gaming and R-rated movie watching 1 year later')

FT_IC2.title: Radiofrequency electromagnetic fields exposure, screen time and cognitive functi
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Primary exposure is radiofrequency electromagnetic fields (RF-EMF), not qualifying smartphone/SM variable. Screen time assessed but RF-EMF is main independent variable of interest. Per B5: RF-EMF → NOT_MET for FT-IC2.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Not evaluated due to IC1 not met. However, paper does assess screen time (mobile phones, tablets, laptops) and RF-EMF exposure.

FT_IC2.title: Problematic smartphone use: What can teenagers and parents do to reduce use?
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Article discusses smartphone use and reduction strategies but does not conduct original quantitative SM/phone analysis.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: While the article discusses smartphone use and reduction strategies, it is not an original empirical study. It is a perspective piece commenting on other research. No original qualifying exposure variable measurement.

FT_IC2.title: Trajectories of psychotic-like experiences in youth and associations with lifest
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Abstract mentions 'baseline lifestyle behaviors (including self-reported screen time, physical activity and caffeine intake...)' and results show 'individuals in the Persistent Elevated Distress trajectory spent more time using screens (adjusted Odds Ratio [OR] 2.27, 95% confidence interval [CI] 2.03-2.53)'. Screen time is unspecified-platform generic measure. Per B5 (unspecified-platform 'internet use' without device specification → NOT_MET) and B7 ('reading-on-screens, e-mail' and generic screen time without SM/smartphone breakdown → NOT_MET). No mention of smartphone ownership, app use, social media, or texting as qualifying exposures.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Self-reported screen time (qualifying smartphone/screen variable per B5: time on phone/screen time is qualifying exposure). Also includes physical activity, caffeine, sleep - but screen time is the qualifying exposure.

FT_IC2.title: Associations between media parenting practices and early adolescent consumption
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Parental screen time practices (bedroom screen use, mealtime screen use, parental monitoring) constitute smartphone/screen-based device use exposure; applies B1 (YSTS papers reporting separate coefficients for distinct screen behaviors)
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Only R-rated movies and mature-rated video games examined. No separately-measured smartphone or social media exposure with own coefficient. TV and gaming alone fail B5 (TV-only, console/unspecified gaming without SM per separate coefficient)

FT_IC2.title: Higher recreational screen time and lower step count are associated with higher
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Exposure variables are 'screen time' (self-reported hours/day watching TV/videos, gaming, texting, social media, video chat) and 'physical activity' (Fitbit step count). Screen time is unspecified-platform general media use without device/platform breakdown and without separate SM coefficient. Per B5, 'unspecified-platform gaming' and generic 'internet use' without device/platform specificity do NOT qualify. No separate smartphone or SM variable reported independently. Paper does not report separate coefficients for smartphone ownership, SM platform-specific use, or qualifying SM metrics.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Recreational screen time - qualifying smartphone/screen use variable: self-reported total hours per day of screen use (watching television, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat) categorized as low (0-4), medium (>4-8), or high (>8) hours/day per B1

FT_IC2.title: 24-Hour Movement Behaviors and Impulsivity.
FT_IC2.c1_evidence: Recreational screen time (ST) measured via Youth Screen Time Survey. Screen time measured as continuous variable in hours. Meets B1: ST as independent variable with regression coefficient reported. Also assesses sleep (9–11 hours per night recommendation) and physical activity (≥60 min/day) in relation to impulsivity dimensions.
FT_IC2.c2_evidence: Screen time measured as recreational ST (≤2 hours/day guideline recommendation) but NOT reported with separate qualifying coefficients. ST is bundled into combined movement behavior recommendation categories (PA+ST+sleep, ST+sleep, etc.). No individual ST coefficient reported independently. Violates B1 requirement for separate per-item breakdown. Screen time appears only as component of aggregate behavior recommendation compliance, not as separately analyzed qualifying variable.

FT_EC7_applies.title: Childhood internalizing, externalizing and attention symptoms predict changes in
FT_EC7_applies.c1_evidence: FAILS condition (a): Paper has BOTH directions - psychiatric symptoms → screen time AND screen time → psychiatric symptoms. Models assess psychiatric symptoms as predictors of screen time AND screen time as predictors of psychiatric symptoms. Applies B18: bidirectional analysis disqualifies FT-EC7.
FT_EC7_applies.c2_evidence: BINDING RULE B20: Paper tests bidirectional/cross-lagged associations. Abstract states 'Comparable magnitude associations were observed for social and nonsocial media use predicting future psychiatric symptoms, suggesting bidirectionality.' Models include both psychiatric symptoms→screen use AND screen use→psychiatric symptoms pathways. FT-EC7 disqualifies bidirectional analyses per binding rule.

FT_EC7_applies.title: Youth screen use in the ABCDÂ® study.
FT_EC7_applies.c1_evidence: Smartphone/SM is the DEPENDENT variable in all analyses: time on SM, SMAQ, video gaming time, VGAQ are outcomes. Predictors are exclusively demographics: sex, race/ethnicity, age (per Table 1, Methods). All analyses are longitudinal/prospective (baseline to Year 1 to Year 2). Paper has NO SM↔non-SM outcome analysis. Satisfies FT-EC7 binding conditions (a), (b), (c) with no cross-sectional demographic-SM carve-out.
FT_EC7_applies.c2_evidence: Paper fails FT_IC2 and FT_IC5 (measurement-only); EC7 logic does not apply.

FT_EC7_applies.title: Identification and characterization of screen use trajectories from late childho
FT_EC7_applies.c1_evidence: Fails FT_IC2 binding rule (B2); screen use is DV (not IV) but only aggregated summed measure available; even if SM were IV, cross-sectional perception of discrimination measure (year 1) mixed with longitudinal trajectory violates longitudinal-only requirement for FT-EC7
FT_EC7_applies.c2_evidence: SATISFIES FT-EC7: (a) Screen use is the DEPENDENT variable in all analyses (growth mixture modeling identifies trajectory groups as outcome); no analyses with screen use as predictor of developmental outcomes. (b) Predictors of trajectory group membership are exclusively demographic: race/ethnicity, parental education, household income—all within the closed demographic-only set. (c) All analyses are LONGITUDINAL (prospective trajectories from age 10-14 with year-1 perceived discrimination outcome). No cross-sectional carve-out applies (B15).

FT_EC7_applies.title: ACEs, Problematic Social Media Use, and Emotional/Behavioral Dysregulation Among
FT_EC7_applies.c1_evidence: ACEs (adverse childhood experiences) is an explicit disqualifier per B17 (parental psychopathology, family conflict, ACEs, etc. as predictors of SM). Additionally, SM is not the dependent variable; rather, PSMU is outcome being predicted by ACEs.
FT_EC7_applies.c2_evidence: FT_EC7 = TRUE. All three conditions met: (a) PSMU is the DEPENDENT variable in all analyses (ACEs → PSMU model); (b) Predictors are demographic-only: cumulative ACEs (childhood adversity exposure), age, gender, race/ethnicity, parent marital status, parent education, household income—all in closed demographic set per B15 criterion; (c) All analyses are LONGITUDINAL (Y1 ACEs predicting Y3 PSMU with 3-year prospective follow-up). No SM-as-IV → outcome analysis present. No cross-sectional demo→SM reported. No ACE confounders (parenting, psychopathology, sleep, depression, anxiety, etc.) excluded per B17. Cross-sectional carve-out (B15) does not apply—all analyses are prospective. Condition (b) satisfied: ACEs are demographic (adversity), not psychological mechanisms.

FT_EC7_applies.title: Pet Ownership and Family Involvement in Sports and Other Activities.
FT_EC7_applies.c1_evidence: Meets all three FT-EC7 conditions: (a) Screen time IS the dependent variable in every analysis; no SM→outcome analyses present; (b) All predictors are demographic/contextual only (pet ownership, demographic covariates, contextual covariates per bioecological model); (c) Longitudinal/prospective design. This is a demographic→SM DV study with no external outcomes, qualifying for exclusion under FT-EC7.
FT_EC7_applies.c2_evidence: Paper fails FT-IC2 (no qualifying smartphone/SM exposure with its own extracted coefficient); screen time is controlled but not analyzed as primary exposure

FT_EC7_applies.title: Social epidemiology of online dating in U.S. early adolescents.
FT_EC7_applies.c1_evidence: All three FT-EC7 conditions hold: (a) Online dating is the DEPENDENT variable in every analysis (demographics → SM); no SM → non-SM-outcome analysis present. (b) All predictors are demographic-only (sex, race/ethnicity, age, household income, parental education, sexual orientation). (c) Cross-sectional data only (Year 2, 2018-2020). B15 carve-out does not apply (no longitudinal/prospective analysis present). FT-EC7 = TRUE.
FT_EC7_applies.c2_evidence: Cross-sectional analysis with demographic predictors of SM (online dating), but FT-EC7 requires LONGITUDINAL analyses only (condition c fails). Also does not meet condition (a): SM is IV (predictor) of outcomes, not DV. Paper tests sociodemographic → online dating associations, not a longitudinal demographic-only → SM study. Per B15: cross-sectional carve-out applies.

FT_EC7_applies.title: Sociodemographic Associations With Early Smartphone Ownership in US Adolescents
FT_EC7_applies.c1_evidence: Cross-sectional logistic regression design (B15 carve-out applies: FT_EC7 does NOT apply when ANY cross-sectional analysis with demographics → SM is reported). Paper uses cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression, not longitudinal/prospective analysis.
FT_EC7_applies.c2_evidence: All three FT-EC7 conditions likely met: (a) Smartphone is DV (demographics → early SM ownership), no SM→outcome analysis; (b) Predictors appear to be sociodemographics (sociodemographic associations); (c) Longitudinal structure inferred from 'early ownership' framing in ABCD prospective cohort. Applies B17 check—no disqualifying psychosocial/behavioral predictors evident in abstract. No cross-sectional/cross-lagged/bidirectional indicators. FT-EC7 = TRUE.

FT_IC3.title: The association between adverse childhood experiences (ACEs), bullying victimiza
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Linear regression models examining ACEs/bullying → internalizing/externalizing outcomes with coefficients reported (Table 2).
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Not evaluated; fails FT-IC2

FT_IC3.title: Associations Between Adverse Childhood Experiences, Adolescent Screen Time and P
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Linear regression analyses with ACE score predictor and screen time outcome; multiple t-tests, correlation coefficients reported (Table 2, Figure 1)
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Does not qualify; IC2 not met. Paper uses linear regression examining ACE → screen time association, but screen time is outcome not exposure.

FT_IC3.title: The Association between Family Environment and Subsequent Risk of Cyberbullying
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Multivariable logistic regression analyses. OR and 95% CI reported for associations between parental monitoring and family conflict at Year 1 and cyberbullying victimization at Year 2 (e.g., OR = 0.61, 95% CI 0.50–0.75 for parental monitoring; OR = 1.10, 95% CI 1.04–1.16 for family conflict).
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Not applicable; FT_IC2 not met

FT_IC3.title: Prevalence and Patterns of Social Media Use in Early Adolescents.
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Purely descriptive statistics. Paper reports prevalence percentages and Chi-square/t-tests for demographic stratification (age, sex) but NO inferential test linking SM variables to external outcome. No regression coefficient, correlation, or statistical test of SM predicting a non-SM outcome. Per B10—SM is only characterized descriptively, not tested against external outcome.
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Chi-square tests and t-tests for comparisons across demographic characteristics; descriptive proportions and statistical tests comparing groups by age and sex

FT_IC3.title: Identification and Validation of Distinct Latent Neurodevelopmental Profiles in
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: No SM ↔ non-SM inferential tests. The paper is a latent profile analysis (LPA) of brain activation patterns. Screen time is included as a covariate in demographic comparisons (ANOVA, χ² tests) but is not the primary exposure in any analysis testing associations with behavioral or clinical outcomes. Brain activation is the DV; demographics and screen time are characteristics of profiles.
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Latent profile analysis with inferential comparisons; chi-square tests and F-tests comparing profiles on screen time (χ²=6.80, p<.0001)

FT_IC3.title: Youth screen use in the ABCDÂ® study.
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: T-tests comparing sex differences (p < .001 for girls higher SMAQ, boys higher VGAQ); chi-squared tests; ANOVA; CFA/factor analysis (χ2 tests for invariance across sex/race); linear mixed-effects models; descriptive statistics with p-values
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: B12: Only SM-vs-SM concordance (factor invariance testing, SMAQ/VGAQ equivalence across groups). No inferential test of SM ↔ non-SM-outcome relationship.

FT_IC3.title: NowIKnowMyABCD: A global resource hub for researchers using data from the ABCD S
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: No empirical statistical analyses; this is a documentation/resource paper
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Paper reports no inferential statistical tests on SM or smartphone data. Descriptive resource paper only.

FT_IC3.title: Association of cyberbullying victimization and substance initiation: The Adolesc
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Multivariable logistic regression; cross-sectional analysis at Year 2 and longitudinal analysis Year 2 → Year 3; odds ratios (OR) and 95% CIs reported.
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: No SM-specific coefficient; cyberbullying is sole exposure, predicting substance initiation; no SM ↔ non-SM outcome coefficient

FT_IC3.title: Radiofrequency electromagnetic fields, screen time, and emotional and behavioura
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Does not meet FT-IC1; evaluation stops. Paper uses logistic regression with ORs (e.g., TV ≥1.5 h/day: OR 3.13, 95%CI 1.43-6.82) but population exclusion is binding disqualification.
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Logistic regression models conducted; adjusted ORs with 95% CIs reported for RF-EMF sources and screen time exposures vs emotional/behavioral outcomes (Tables 2-3).

FT_IC3.title: Adherence to 24-Hour Movement Recommendations and Health Indicators in Early Ado
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models comparing adherence categories to cognitive, psychosocial, and morphometric outcomes; cross-sectional (T1) and longitudinal (T1-T2) analyses reported with regression coefficients.
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: No SM-specific inferential test; only categorical adherence groups (met/not-met combinations) compared via multilevel linear models; SM not treated as separate exposure variable

FT_IC3.title: Cyberbullying, mental health, and substance use experimentation among early adol
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Not assessed due to FT_IC2 NOT_MET status
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Logistic and linear regression analyses with adjusted odds ratios and coefficients

FT_IC3.title: Identification and characterization of screen use trajectories from late childho
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Fails FT_IC2; no separate inference tests on SM variables
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Growth mixture modeling used to identify subgroups with different screen use trajectories, with statistical comparisons of class membership by sociodemographic characteristics and perceived racism/discrimination outcomes. Inferential tests: ANOVA/regression testing differences between trajectory groups.

FT_IC3.title: Prospective associations of sleep duration and screen time with transition from
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: While Cox proportional hazards models are used, FT-IC2 is NOT_MET, making this moot for inclusion
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Cox proportional hazards regression; HRs with 95% CIs and p-values reported for prospective associations with BMI transition

FT_IC3.title: Generalizable prediction of childhood ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive testing
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Fails FT_IC2; screen time is treated as a feature in predictive model, not as independent variable in inferential test of SM associations
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Machine learning predictive models (Lasso/Ridge penalized regression, PCR with multiple imputation) predict ADHD symptoms at 1-year and 2-year follow-up. Traditional regression coefficients also reported as baseline comparisons. Multiple cross-validated model evaluations with quantified prediction accuracy (r values, correlation coefficients between predicted and actual ADHD scores).

FT_IC3.title: Independent and Joint Prospective Associations of Screen Time and Sleep Disturba
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Weighted multivariable linear regression models used but FT-IC2 NOT_MET makes this moot for inclusion
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Weighted multivariable linear regression models; B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for independent associations; joint associations tested with categorical groupings

FT_IC3.title: Problematic smartphone use: What can teenagers and parents do to reduce use?
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Opinion piece; no inferential testing of associations presented.
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: No original inferential test. This is a perspective/commentary with references to other studies' findings.

FT_IC3.title: Traumatic Stress Exposure as a Predictor of Dual Systems Model Development: Exam
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with path coefficients and indirect effects reported. Example: traumatic stress exposure → deviant peer association → impulse control mediation pathway; coefficient for mediated effect reported as 'accounting for about 25% of this relationship' with significance testing (p-values and confidence intervals provided in Tables 2 and 3).
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Not applicable; FT_IC2 not met

FT_IC3.title: Associations between media parenting practices and early adolescent consumption
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Ordinal logistic regression models with adjusted odds ratios for associations between media parenting practices and R-rated movies/mature video games
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Paper does not meet FT-IC2; IC2 failure precludes IC3 consideration

FT_IC3.title: Sociodemographic Associations With Early Smartphone Ownership in US Adolescents
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Multivariable logistic regression model examining associations between sociodemographic predictors and early smartphone ownership (<10 years) as binary outcome; odds ratios calculated
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Abstract snippet provided is extremely limited (truncated to ~20 words). Based on title and abstract context, study appears to examine sociodemographic associations WITH smartphone ownership (demographics → smartphone), not smartphone → outcome. No test of smartphone ↔ non-SM outcome mentioned in available text. Likely not an inferential test of SM effect on a distal outcome.

FT_IC3.title: Passive Sensing of Preteens' Smartphone Use: An Adolescent Brain Cognitive Devel
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: No inferential test of SM ↔ non-SM outcome. Paper reports descriptive statistics (means, SDs), correlations between self-report and objective sensing data (r=0.49), and ANOVA for demographic differences in app use. This is a measurement validation/feasibility substudy. Applies B12 carve-out: SM-vs-objective concordance only, no SM → outcome analysis.
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Inferential tests reported. Pearson correlations between passive sensing data and self/parent-reported screen time (r=0.49). Analyses of variance comparing app use by demographics. Two-sample t-tests examining differences in app use. Paper states 'Analyses of variance were run to assess differences in categorical app use by demographics.'

FT_IC3.title: ADHD and gaming addiction in adolescents: psychosocial mediators in the adolesce
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Dependent on IC2. Regression models (linear mixed-effects and parallel mediation) are present, but no SM/smartphone outcome inference tested. ADHD is IV, gaming addiction (VGAQ) is DV.
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models testing ADHD diagnosis→VGAQ scores longitudinally; parallel mediation model with multiple mediators; indirect and direct effects computed

FT_IC3.title: Typologies of Family Functioning and 24-h Movement Behaviors.
FT_IC3.c1_evidence: Logistic regression models with OR, 95% CI for family typologies predicting movement behavior recommendations met (Table 3, p. 6)
FT_IC3.c2_evidence: Screen time is outcome, not exposure; no inferential test of SM ↔ non-SM outcome. No relevant SM exposure analysis.

FT_IC4.title: The association between adverse childhood experiences (ACEs), bullying victimiza
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Multiple β coefficients with 95% CI reported in Table 2 for main effects and interaction models.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Not evaluated; fails FT-IC2

FT_IC4.title: Associations Between Adverse Childhood Experiences, Adolescent Screen Time and P
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: β coefficients with 95% CI reported in Table 2 for ACE-screen time associations (e.g., unadjusted β=0.54 [0.36,0.71], adjusted β=0.44 [0.26,0.62]); p-values (<.001)
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Does not qualify; IC2 not met.

FT_IC4.title: The Association between Family Environment and Subsequent Risk of Cyberbullying
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Numeric extractable: OR, 95% CI, and p-values reported in Tables 2 and 3 for both mean scores and individual survey items. Example: parental monitoring OR = 0.61 (0.50, 0.75); family conflict OR = 1.10 (1.04, 1.16). Secondary analyses with individual items also include coefficients and CIs.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Not applicable; FT_IC2 not met

FT_IC4.title: Prevalence and Patterns of Social Media Use in Early Adolescents.
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: No regression coefficients, OR, RR, HR, d, r, F, t, χ², η², R², p-values, or CI reported for SM↔outcome associations. Only descriptive percentages and prevalence data in tables.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Percentages, proportions, mean hours/day, Chi-square p-values, and t-test p-values reported in Table 1 and text

FT_IC4.title: Youth screen use in the ABCDÂ® study.
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Tables 1, 4, 5 report χ2 values, p-values, RMSEA, CFI, SRMR; Figures 1–3 show proportions with 95% CIs; t-values and p-values reported throughout (p < .001 for multiple comparisons)
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: No extractable numeric SM ↔ outcome coefficient. Chi-square and p-values reported for factor structure comparisons, but these test measurement invariance, not SM predicting behavioral/health outcomes.

FT_IC4.title: Association of cyberbullying victimization and substance initiation: The Adolesc
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: ORs and 95% CIs reported in Tables 2 (main text): e.g., OR=2.17 [1.68, 2.81] for lifetime cyberbullying; OR=2.22 [1.68, 2.93] for longitudinal association.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: No SM numeric coefficient to extract; only cyberbullying OR reported

FT_IC4.title: Outdoor and indoor sources of residential radiofrequency electromagnetic fields,
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Regression coefficients and standard errors reported in text and supplementary tables; e.g., results for RF-EMF exposure associations with cognitive outcomes in main results section
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Abstract does not provide numeric coefficients, p-values, or CIs. States qualitative associations ('associated with improved', 'reduced') but no extractable β/OR/p/CI. PDF would clarify if numeric values are in tables/methods.

FT_IC4.title: Radiofrequency electromagnetic fields, screen time, and emotional and behavioura
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Paper fails on FT-IC1. While numeric values are extractable (ORs, CIs reported in results), the population criterion failure is binding.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Adjusted OR, 95% CI reported in Tables 2-3 for mobile phone base stations, cordless phone, WiFi, mobile/cordless phone calls, and screen time exposure associations with teacher/maternal-reported emotional and behavioral problems.

FT_IC4.title: The Pandemic's Toll on Young Adolescents: Prevention and Intervention Targets to
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Reports SHAP values (mean absolute SHAP) for feature importance (Figures 1–4). Per B8: traditional regression coefficients appear in linear mixed-effects models and GBT outputs. Supplementary materials include AUC values, p-values
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: B8/B9 (ML rule): Only SHAP feature importance values reported (mean absolute SHAP values for top 20 features per outcome model). No traditional regression coefficient (β, OR, RR, t, χ², p-value) anywhere in main text or supplement for individual screen time variables predicting outcomes. SHAP values are feature importance rankings, not comparable coefficients. No sensitivity analysis or supplement reporting traditional regression.

FT_IC4.title: Adherence to 24-Hour Movement Recommendations and Health Indicators in Early Ado
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Standardized regression coefficients (β), 95% CIs, and p-values reported in main text (Figure 2, Tables); multiple coefficients for different 24-hour movement combinations.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: No numeric extraction possible for SM; categorical grouping only (not individual coefficient for SM ↔ outcome)

FT_IC4.title: Cyberbullying, mental health, and substance use experimentation among early adol
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Not assessed due to FT_IC2 NOT_MET status
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Adjusted odds ratios with 95% CI reported: cyberbullying→alcohol AOR=1.98 (95% CI 1.53-2.57), nicotine AOR=3.37 (95% CI 2.16-5.26), cannabis AOR=4.65 (95% CI 2.46-8.77)

FT_IC4.title: Identification and characterization of screen use trajectories from late childho
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Fails FT_IC2; no extractable coefficients for individual SM measures
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Multiple numeric values reported: N values for trajectory groups (N=1,333 Drastically Increasing; N=10,336 Gradually Increasing), percentages for racial/ethnic composition and perceived discrimination experiences (e.g., 70% vs 45% racial/ethnic minorities, 8% vs 3% perceived unfair treatment by teachers), presented in Table 1 and main text.

FT_IC4.title: One-year predictions of delayed reward discounting in the adolescent brain cogni
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Machine learning models report feature importance; applies B8 rule: 'Penalized regression β (Lasso/Ridge/elastic-net) counts.' Elastic net regression explicitly used, permitting extraction of regularized coefficients from main models.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Abstract does not provide numeric model coefficients or performance metrics. Requires full paper/supplement to confirm whether β values from elastic net or other numeric statistics are reported

FT_IC4.title: Prospective associations of sleep duration and screen time with transition from
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Numeric values present but FT-IC2 is NOT_MET, making this moot
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Hazard ratios, 95% confidence intervals, p-values, and trend tests reported in Table 2 and main text; standardized estimates in sensitivity analyses (Table 3)

FT_IC4.title: Generalizable prediction of childhood ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive testing
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Fails FT_IC2; no extractable coefficients specific to screen time as primary exposure of interest
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Extensive numeric results: predictive model explained 15-20% of variance in 1-year ADHD symptoms (PCR r=0.46, CI 0.42-0.47; LASSO r=0.45, CI 0.42-0.48), 12-13% in 2-year symptoms. Figure 1 displays r-values for multiple model iterations. Figure 3 shows feature weights. Supplementary material includes detailed model coefficients and comparison statistics.

FT_IC4.title: Pet Ownership and Family Involvement in Sports and Other Activities.
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Adjusted odds ratios with 95% CI (e.g., AOR=1.167, 95% CI: 0.957-1.424); McFadden's Pseudo R², F-statistics reported in main text.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Tables 2-3 report coefficients (ORs, β, 95% CIs, p-values) for pet ownership and outcomes (physical activity, sports involvement, screen time), but screen time values not clearly extracted as separate numeric results

FT_IC4.title: Independent and Joint Prospective Associations of Screen Time and Sleep Disturba
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Numeric values present but FT-IC2 NOT_MET makes this moot
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: B coefficients (0.32, 0.40, etc.), 95% confidence intervals, p-values reported in Tables 2-3 for screen time and sleep associations with BMI and WC

FT_IC4.title: What we know about screen time and social media in early adolescence: a review o
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Review article; does not directly report numeric coefficients from the paper's own analyses. References other ABCD studies with coefficients but does not present original analyses with extractable statistics. Full text not provided for all sections; abstract states it reviews 'current ABCD Study data' but review format typically does not include original numeric reporting
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Summarizes numeric findings from ABCD analyses (e.g., effect sizes, associations with mental health outcomes).

FT_IC4.title: Confirmatory Factor Analysis on Mental Health Status using ABCD Cohort
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Paper reports only aggregate variance explained (8.02% for all predictors combined) with no individual coefficients for screen time extracted. Per B2 rule: summed/total effect reported, no per-item breakdown of SM coefficient separately.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Variance explained by mixed-effects model = 8.02%; correlation coefficients r=0.87 (p factor models), r=0.32 (internalizing), r=0.02 (externalizing); CFA fit indices (CFI=0.957/0.948, TLI=0.928/0.927, RMSEA=0.112/0.060)

FT_IC4.title: Problematic smartphone use: What can teenagers and parents do to reduce use?
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: No original numeric data extractable from this paper.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: While percentages are cited (e.g., 95% used reduction strategies, 78.1% used silent mode), these come from referenced studies, not original analysis reported in this article.

FT_IC4.title: Bidirectional associations of problematic social media use and problematic gamin
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Cross-lagged panel model coefficients reported. Standardized estimates provided (e.g., alpha measures of SMDS, IGDS scales; regression coefficients from CLPM). Path analysis with statistical significance testing
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: PDF available but abstract only provided in batch. Full text required to extract numeric values (beta, OR, correlation coefficients)

FT_IC4.title: Traumatic Stress Exposure as a Predictor of Dual Systems Model Development: Exam
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Numeric coefficients and p-values extracted from SEM analyses. Tables 2 and 3 report standardized regression coefficients, 95% confidence intervals, and p-values for all direct and indirect effects examined (e.g., coefficient = .339, p < .001 for sensation-seeking model; coefficient = .142, p = .028 for impulse control).
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Not applicable; FT_IC2 not met

FT_IC4.title: Prevalence and Correlates of Emotion Dysregulation in Children: Results from the
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Numeric data available (aOR, p-values) but not for qualifying SM variable per IC2 definition
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Numeric data (ORs) provided for demographic, sleep, and psychiatric correlates of ED, but no extractable coefficients for SM/screen time relationships

FT_IC4.title: Associations between media parenting practices and early adolescent consumption
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Adjusted odds ratios (95% confidence intervals) reported in Table 2, e.g., AOR 1.44 [1.36–1.53] for bedroom screen use and R-rated movies
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Paper does not meet FT-IC2

FT_IC4.title: Sociodemographic Associations With Early Smartphone Ownership in US Adolescents
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Multiple OR and 95% CI reported in Table 2: e.g., Female OR=1.38 (95% CI 1.19–1.60); Black OR=2.78 (95% CI 2.07–3.64); Single parent OR=2.01 (95% CI 1.67–2.44); Parental education <Bachelor's OR=1.70 (95% CI 1.40–2.06)
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: No numeric data (β, OR, p, CI) in available abstract excerpt. PDF would be required to assess.

FT_IC4.title: ADHD and gaming addiction in adolescents: psychosocial mediators in the adolesce
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Numeric results available (β = 1.29 [95% CI: 0.97, 1.61], p < 0.0001 for ADHD on VGAQ; mediation percentages 7.4%, 5.1%, 5.8%), but not for qualifying SM/smartphone variable per IC2
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Beta coefficients, 95% CIs, p-values reported in Table 3 and supplementary tables; indirect effects and proportion of effect mediated in Table 4; Figure 2 shows path coefficients

FT_IC4.title: Typologies of Family Functioning and 24-h Movement Behaviors.
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Odds ratios with 95% CI reported in Table 3 (p. 6). Example: Family typology P2 OR=0.54, 95% CI 0.39-0.76 for meeting all three recommendations
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: N/A—no SM exposure analysis present.

FT_IC4.title: American Children's Screen Time: Diminished Returns of Household Income in Black
FT_IC4.c1_evidence: Abstract does not report specific numeric coefficients (β, OR, p, CI). States 'weaker inverse association' in Black vs. White families and mentions 'significant statistical interaction' but no numeric extraction from abstract alone. PDF not read for numeric detail.
FT_IC4.c2_evidence: Abstract and methods provided but no numeric coefficients, effect sizes, or confidence intervals extracted from the paper text. Results section absent from provided content. Cannot verify whether β, OR, p-values, or CI are reported in main text or tables.

FT_IC6.title: The association between adverse childhood experiences (ACEs), bullying victimiza
FT_IC6.c1_evidence: No evidence of duplicate; appears as original research article.

FT_IC6.title: The Association between Family Environment and Subsequent Risk of Cyberbullying
FT_IC6.c1_evidence: Published article in Academic Pediatrics (2024); no evidence of duplication.

FT_IC6.title: Cyberbullying, mental health, and substance use experimentation among early adol
FT_IC6.c1_evidence: Not assessed due to FT_IC2 NOT_MET status

FT_IC6.title: Bidirectional associations of problematic social media use and problematic gamin
FT_IC6.c1_evidence: Published empirical article (Journal of Research on Adolescence, 2025); no evidence of preprint/abstract/duplicate
FT_IC6.c2_evidence: No duplicate markers evident but insufficient information in abstract

FT_IC6.title: Traumatic Stress Exposure as a Predictor of Dual Systems Model Development: Exam
FT_IC6.c1_evidence: Published article in Crime & Delinquency (2024); no evidence of duplication.

FT_IC6.title: Associations between media parenting practices and early adolescent consumption
